headline: The East–West direction receives 14% more demand than North–South, but the fixed signal split gives it insufficient green time; meanwhile, the pedestrian phase is consuming 20% of the cycle, holding back vehicle traffic during peak periods.
primary_cause: Misaligned signal split (fixed timing does not match directional demand imbalance)
recommendation: Reprogram the signal controller to increase green time on the East–West axis (currently insufficient for the 1.14× demand imbalance). Monitor the pedestrian phase; if cross-traffic demand continues to build during peak hours (1200–2400 s window), consider creating a dedicated pedestrian-exclusive phase outside the vehicle green window, or shorten the all-way pedestrian signal. Implement adaptive (time-of-day or demand-responsive) signal timing to shift green allocation as demand patterns change throughout the day.
expected_impact: Smart signal timing typically reduces average vehicle wait time by 15–30%, depending on demand profile. At this junction, with sustained peak queues of 91.95 vehicles and 119 s average wait, a 20% reduction would save drivers ~24 seconds of delay per trip.
justification: The East–West average queue (37.4 vehicles) exceeds North–South (32.69 vehicles) by 14% (imbalance ratio 1.14), yet per-approach queues are nearly balanced (N: 16.41, S: 16.28, E: 18.53, W: 18.87). This points to a signal-split mismatch rather than random fluctuation. The pedestrian backup ratio of 1.21 shows vehicle queues rise 21% during pedestrian crossing phases (81.27 veh vs. 67.27 veh during vehicle-only phases), confirming that the pedestrian signal allocation (20.1% of cycle) is compressing vehicle green time. Peak congestion builds steadily from 0–2400 s and plateaus at ~88–92 vehicles, indicating sustained suppressed throughput. With 119 s average wait and 70 vehicles in queue, the signal is unable to clear demand efficiently. Parking is set to 0 because no parking/encroachment report was provided.
temporal_note: Congestion builds over the first 40 minutes (0–2400 s) and holds steady at peak levels for 3 hours before declining after 3600 s. The surge corresponds to mid-day weekend activity. A time-of-day plan should allocate more green to East–West during this 2-hour window.
